title: Dense Retrieval in Practice
concept: Embedding both the query and documents, then ranking by vector similarity as the core dense retrieval loop.

exercises.prompt: What is a major limitation of traditional keyword-based search?

options.text: It fails when queries use different words than the document to describe the same concept.
It is too computationally expensive to run on modern computers.
It requires converting all text into binary machine code manually.

exercises.explain: Since this is the first lesson of the unit on retrieval types, this hook question establishes the limitation of simple keyword searching, which fails when users use different words to describe the same concept.

cards.title: Beyond Simple Keyword Search
cards.body: Traditional search engines fail if you don't type the exact right keyword. Dense retrieval solves this by understanding the deeper meaning and intent behind your search, opening up a whole new level of semantic search.

cards.title: What is Dense Retrieval?
cards.body: Dense retrieval finds relevant documents by converting both the query and the documents into lists of numbers called vector embeddings. It then ranks documents based on how close their vectors are in high-dimensional space, capturing meaning rather than exact words.

cards.title: The Personality Matchmaker
cards.body: Think of dense retrieval like matching people at a masquerade ball by their personalities instead of their costumes. Even if they don't say the exact same words, they dance together because they share the same vibe and interests.

cards.title: Dense Retrieval in Action
cards.body: If a user searches for 'canine therapy', a dense retriever can easily find documents about 'dogs helping patients' because the embeddings for these concepts live close together in the vector space, even though they share no matching words.

cards.title: The Keyword Fallacy
cards.body: Many believe dense retrieval requires matching exact words. In reality, it doesn't look for keyword matches at all; it relies entirely on the semantic relationships captured in the vector coordinates.

cards.title: Keyword vs. Semantic Space
cards.body: Comparing how search methods interpret the relationship between queries and documents.

left.text: Looks for exact word overlaps like 'dog' and 'dog'. Misses synonyms.

right.text: Compares coordinates in vector space. Matches 'puppy' to 'canine' easily.

exercises.prompt: What are the vector embeddings used in dense retrieval?

options.text: A list of all misspelled words in the text.
Numerical vectors that represent the semantic meaning of text.
Hyperlinks that connect different documents together.
A dictionary of exact keyword counts.

exercises.explain: Embeddings are mathematical vectors (lists of numbers) that capture the semantic meaning of text, allowing us to compare their similarity.

exercises.prompt: True or False: In dense retrieval, both the search query and the candidate documents must be converted into vector embeddings.
exercises.explain: Both the query and the documents must be converted into embeddings (vectors) so that their similarity can be mathematically calculated.

exercises.prompt: Fill in the blank to complete the explanation of dense retrieval mechanics:
exercises.sentence: In dense retrieval, we rank documents based on their vector similarity to the search query.
exercises.explain: Dense retrieval relies on vector similarity metrics (like cosine distance) to determine how close the query's meaning is to each document's meaning in the embedding space.

exercises.prompt: Identify the correct explanation of the dense retrieval process:

options.text: Dense retrieval works by converting only the query into a vector and comparing it to raw, un-embedded text files using string search.
In dense retrieval, you must use one embedding model for the query and a completely different type of model for the documents to prevent them from overlapping.
To perform dense retrieval, we pass both the query and the documents through an embedding model and rank the documents based on their similarity scores.

exercises.explain: Option C is written in an AI-like style with clean, direct structure, correctly explaining that dense retrieval embeds both the query and documents before comparing them. Options A and B contain factual errors or confusion about how embedding models are used.

exercises.prompt: Which of the following describes the complete, end-to-end loop of a dense retrieval system?

options.text: Embed the query and documents with the same model, calculate vector similarity, and rank the results.
Search for exact keyword matches, count word frequencies, and sort them alphabetically.
Translate the query into SQL, query a relational database, and sort by date.
Generate a summary of the query, ask an LLM to write a new document, and return that document.

exercises.explain: The complete dense retrieval loop involves converting both the user query and the corpus documents into vector embeddings using the same model, then calculating similarity (like cosine similarity) to find and rank the closest matches.
